speaker: interviewer
text: Interviewing Janet Rogowsky. May 3rd, 1996. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were talking about the actions.

speaker: interviewee
text: And talking about that my sister fi-- When she heard this announcement, ran to the apartment to see what's happening with Mother. She probably would have gone with my mother. She was a very courageous girl, my sister. I really loved her very much. She was-- I was very close to her. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I should have never said it. So she came in. And we lived at that time in an area-- you asked me before if we lived in a ghetto. We never did, thanks to the lawyer, because he already made out for us papers. He saw that things were getting bad, and that we should have prepared papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and he found for us a one particular apartment house that was between the ghetto and the area inside. It was really on the edge but still, there, Jews were not officially in the ghetto. And I don't know why they even left this particular area. And-- maybe they couldn't surround it by a wall or whatever it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when she came in, we're on the fifth or sixth floor. The door was open. And didn't hear my mother so she started to scream. A woman came out from under the bed. And she hid. She heard this noise so she came from her apartment and ran into mother to find out. And she heard the Nazis coming up. So she ran under the bed.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother had a little baby, so she just closed the curtain and kept the baby inside. And when the Nazis came, they started to push my mother to go down. And my mother showed the baby, so [INAUDIBLE] to take the baby along. So that-- all that my sister found out from that woman because she was there hearing that. When the Nazis left, she came out from under the cover. And she told my sister that they started to push and that they threw-- they threw them down from the fourth-- from the fourth floor, from the third floor. They didn't let them go. [INAUDIBLE] fast, fast. And there was like a cattle truck-- you know, they used to transport cattle-- waiting for them. And they all pushed them out. They're all standing there with the baby. And-- and this was it. So later on, now, I found this apartment. We found this apartment through people who lived in Ostrog and knew our parents very well. They-- we weren't really friends, but the children are friends. We were very good friends with the children. We went to school together. A very nice family. And when they heard that we're in trouble, my mother asked them if we can-- they had a two bedroom apartment, and they were willing to take us in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they gave us one room, and they took one room. And they lived there with a son who was a Jewish policeman. And he even had his girlfriend there, too, so we were all together. And he told me later that they took them to Treblinka. And the-- the weather was very bad. It was pouring all night.

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't let them sit down. They all had to be standing. The kids were destroyed immediately. And I don't want even to tell you how they destroyed-- how they killed the kids. And then they killed everybody else. They took them to Treblinka and this was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the reason I don't-- I'm-- as I'm talking about it, I'm thinking about my sister-in-law. My sister-in-law doesn't know how her little boy was killed. And anytime I make a tape, I always wonder, how will-- how will I manage to show her, or she finds it? And if she ever reads what I say, she'll probably go crazy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I always am very hesitant about telling this story in a fear that she might sometimes, because we are very, very close friends, like sisters. And I always said to David, we will have to erase this part, if I ever say that. And I imagine it's possible. So this was it. So then the lawyer came. We notified him right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you a question. Since you already had papers-- false papers-- and you knew that the actions were going on, did you and your family talk about taking different identities?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were talking all the time that eventually we'll have to do it, but we determined not to do it as long as Mother is with us. Mother could have never survived this in [? Aryan. ?] Because she, first of all, her Polish was not like ours. It was with an accent. And-- and she didn't have this kind of relationship that we had with Christian children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for us, it was-- as a matter of fact, as I told you, to me it was a help. And I'll tell you in a minute how that I always was-- But you knew that your mother--

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew that eventually-- if Mother goes, we said, we will have to do something about it. You see, again, this is frightening that we never said, if Mother goes, let's commit suicide. We said, if Mother goes, let's see what we can do to help ourselves. Everybody was so determined to survive. That is frightening. I know a friend of mine, she saw her father killed. Her first reaction is to pull out the golden teeth, because she can sell them and-- and have something, some money for food. So that was really-- it's unbelievable. So-- so we-- he gave us overnight. He made papers for us, for the three of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: The three of you being your sister-in-law, your sister, and you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And by the way, they earlier-- the policeman's parents disappeared. And later on, we started wondering that he probably knew that this is-- that there's going to be an action, and he wanted to protect his parents and didn't tell us. And you know, in-- in hindsight, I don't blame him. Because he couldn't have protected us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- because we started to wonder, where are they? They didn't say goodbye to us, and we were very close. So obviously-- I don't-- they didn't survive anyway. Later on, they got killed. But everybody was trying to do something for his own, if he could. But that was impossible, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- so we got our papers and decided to go to Germany. Germany was looking-- was looking for Polish girls, because a lot of men were taken to the army and they needed a lot of people in Germany. So you could very easily say that you're Polish and you want to work in Germany, because in Poland you couldn't find jobs so easily. And we always believed in Germany it will be easier because Poles really recognized, much faster, Jews than the Germans. Because the German Jews were so assimilated that it was much more difficult for Germans to distinguish who is a Jew and who is not. While with the Poles, I mean, Jews were not assimilated. They had their own characteristics. They really tried to stay away from Poles-- Jews. They were always afraid of antisemites. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel that way as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I feel now that way? [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, at that time, did you feel that a Pole would identify you as a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even though you felt so assimilated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But I felt that I would be able to-- that I will know how to handle myself. And I will tell you in a minute what happened. So-- so he brought us the papers, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us the name of this lawyer.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. You mean of that I just read?

speaker: interviewer
text: The lawyer who took care of you, and your sister, and your sister-in-law? Made the papers for you, your father's friend.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INTERPOSING VOICES] [INAUDIBLE] made the paper. He was-- for different-- for my sister, he gave his own name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nowotynska.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's N-O-W-O-T-Y-N-S-K-A. He would be Nowotynski, because he's a man.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh my gosh. I just forgot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, maybe it'll come to you later.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I probably have it on the picture on the back. I forgot. It might be Stephan, but I'm not sure. As a matter of fact, later on, my sister and I helped him to establish a law-- a law practice because he was a lawyer in Warsaw. And we were in touch until his death. So-- so we decided to go to Germany, and leave in the morning. And this is the first time at night that my father appeared in my dreams. I never dreamt about my father. And he said to me, let Anya and Nellie go. But you don't go. It was-- he was, like, on the staircase, and there was a tremendous storm on the ocean, and he was pushing the waves away, and said, don't go. You don't go. Stay home and don't go. And I got up in the morning, and I said, I'm not going.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were very, very upset. They said, what are you talking about? You're not going to stay here alone, and we are going. And I said, look, you can say anything you want. I'm not going. And I didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and by the way, I forgot to tell you that, when we were working in the factory, each of us had to wear a Star of David, you know, the [INAUDIBLE]. So at one time-- you know, it's true that we lived in this apartment house, but there was no water there. So you had to go to a well. And so, even if you went down to the door, you had to have this Star of David. And I just forgot. I ran with the pail across the street. And as I tried to get water, I look up and I see those boots. And I see a German guy approaching me. And I'm saying, this is it. He saw me without the star. And I-- then I go on pumping. And he stands in front of me, and I go on. And when I filled up the pail, I-- I smiled, and I walked away. He didn't do anything. So I don't know if he was just stopped to-- maybe he just suspected somewhere from this building, whatever it is. So I still remember those black, shining boots.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they left, the two of them, to Germany. Some-- a Polish custom officer recognized my-- my sister. She became a bleached blonde, and she made the biggest mistake. Because when she was a brunette, it was fine. She looked more Jewish than-- than when she was a brunette. But most of the Polish girls were blonde, so she wanted to be one of them. So he recognized that she was Jewish on the border, and he looked at her papers that were correct. And he caressed her on his head and said, may God be with you. Go on, and go fast. So that was her first encounter.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they went to Germany. She found-- she was placed with a German farmer where she was milking the cows and taking care of the farm. And they did not know, except one mad woman was in the village, running around all day, screaming in German, Hannah is a Jude. Hannah is a Jude. And everybody was laughing. And everybody said, she is mad. This woman is mad. And my sister said, the only woman who knew that I-- recognized that I was Jewish was smarter than all of you, and they thought that she was mad. And my sister-in-law was in another area, and she was working in a factory where there were about five other Jewish girls. And luckily-- it's an ammunition factory-- and they were all very close friends, and some of them survived. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Had any of you ever been to Germany before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before that? No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was just seeing your father in the dream that made you decide not to go, or were there other things that--

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean before the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. No. That made you decide not to go with your sister and sister-in-law.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [INAUDIBLE] This is the first time. My father never appeared to me. I somehow had a feeling. And I just said, I just don't want to go. And, just [INAUDIBLE] like that. And they were very, very upset. First of all, they were worried about me. So-- and I didn't have any money. I had, like, 200 zloty-- would be like $20. And I didn't know what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So of course, none of us took anything with us. But I took just, like, a shopping bag, and-- like a linen shopping bag, and put in a sweater. And I took-- I had a few dollars and-- and I had a prayer book took along with me. A what--

speaker: interviewee
text: A Catholic prayer book.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get it?

speaker: interviewee
text: He brought it for me. I-- first of all, we all started to say to ourselves that we have to see. Someday, we might have to take it off. I mean, we knew already a lot of Jewish girls became Catholic-- Christians and [INAUDIBLE] hid their identity.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you all share this kind of information?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean, among ourselves?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. But later on, we heard more about it. We just knew that a lot of Jewish girls were caught. From the papers, we heard, they were caught because they made a big mistake. First of all, many of them wearing those big crosses, and that was not-- Polish girls were not wearing big crosses. They were all wearing crosses but smaller ones. And they felt that they, wearing big, everybody will be visible. That was a mistake. And the other mistake some Jewish girls made is they went to hide in sauna. In a-- in a sauna, you know, with a lot of steam. And they fell-- during the day, because they were afraid to walk out. And they started to search the saunas. They started to search the saunas. And they started to search railroad stations because people are running away. So you heard here and there already that something is going on. That they are trying to-- to find more and more Jewish girls who hide their identity.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to the railroad station. At that time, I really didn't know how bad it is. But-- and I didn't know where to go. So I approached the window and I said, can I have a ticket? [INAUDIBLE] said, where to? I said, any place. And that was already the first mistake I made. Because if he really was a mean Pole, he would have right of way notified the Gestapo. So he says, lady, there is no train to any place right now. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I ran through. And at that time, of course, most people traveled with a horse and buggy. So I approached a-- an old coachman, and I said, will you please take me out of town-- out of town? He said, where do you want to go? I said, out of town. And of course, that was the first time. And he was an old man-- toothless, ugly, filthy. And he says, OK, I'll take you, if you give me-- and he-- he mentioned more than what I had. I said, OK. I said to myself, I'll deal with him later.

words.text: He said, where do you want to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: As we drive out from [NON-ENGLISH], he release his seat-- you know, the coach in the seat was elevated-- and comes to sit down next to me. And start taking me around. And I start getting the chills. And then he start telling me, you know, as we go on-- first, he told me about a few towns where I can go, whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, as we go on, I-- I have a friend who has a little cottage, and he's not there during the day. Why don't you and I try to rest there a little bit. I said, that's fine. That's terrific. And as we came, and I said to myself, what am I going to do? Anyway, he's driving. And then he comes to this house. And he says, he's going to the back to see if the door is open, because usually he left him a key in case he's tired. Anyway, he went, and I started to run. And I run. And this was it. And I didn't stop running. Did you know where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And then I ran and I slept. During the day, I came to a-- to a town. And I knew what town that was. I mean, I found out what town that was. And my first thing is, where do I find a place to stay during the day? I'll travel at night. And I see a very nice man on the street. And that was a mistake. You don't approach a very nice man, because those very nice men can be the worst. So I asked him if he know of any place that I could just stay overnight. It doesn't have to be a hotel, because I don't have that much money. He says, yes. As a matter of fact, here's a lady. She-- she rents out rooms. And he showed me, and I thanked him very much. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he said to me, would I like to have a cup of coffee with him, if I don't know anybody. I said, yes. And so he said he'll pick-- I said, give me some time. He says he'll pick me up in a half hour. I says, terrific.

speaker: interviewer
text: As he walks out, the woman comes in and she says to me, you know what he told me? He thinks you're Jewish. I said-- so I started to laugh and giggle and said, that is really funny. And I made it look like nothing. I'm getting ready to sit down and rest. And I said, by the way, can I get a newspaper here? She says, yes, it's downstairs. I'll tell my little boy. He'll bring it to you. I said, no I'll go down and get it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I started to run. I wasn't going to wait for him to take me to the Gestapo. Because if he is-- tells me to come, he'll pick me up. And then he says to her he thinks I'm Jewish, it's bad news. And again I ran. And then, finally, I came to a forest that had-- that was appealing to me. And I decided to sleep under the tree. And of course, there's no food. You don't eat. And you forget that there is no food. Then you start eating leaves from the tree. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: That happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I fell asleep, knocked out. Somebody tapped me on my shoulder. And I look up, and I see a tall Pole standing there. And he said, what are you doing? I said, well, I'm tired. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyways, he knew. He right away knew that I was Jewish. And I said, I'm looking for work. And he said to me, why don't you go? And told me the-- the Austrian [INAUDIBLE]. There is a big farm there. They are always looking for people. Why don't you go and ask them? Maybe they can employ you. So he gave me exactly directions. Told me where not to go, where to go. Didn't tell me that he suspected that I was Jewish. And I went. What makes you think he suspected that you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: What-- that he did suspect I was Jewish?

speaker: interviewer
text: That he thought you were Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, the way he was talking to me. You-- you know right away. How-- why would I know that this guy would have definitely taken me to Gestapo? You know, you realize later what mistake you made. And I guess you have a feeling for-- for those things. You sometimes meet someone, and you say, this guy, if he could, he would kill you. So-- and maybe, I guess, by hearing all kind of stories. And sometimes, you-- you are wrong. But I happened to be right, because the way he was talking to me and told me where I should go. Later on, I started to realized that he suspected-- that's my suspicion. He never even said it, and I never told him that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in the forest before he found you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was going from one to another one. So it was a day, then, you know, wooded areas. And the fields. I was running through the fields very often.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how much time there was went by that you were doing this?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in February.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you cold?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember very cold. I remember I had a sweater. You know, when you run, you are not cold. I don't even remember if I was cold. I don't even remember that I was hungry. I-- you're only scared and you run. So I really don't remember if I was cold. And I came there, and the first thing, an officer-- his secretary is interviewing me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You came to the--

speaker: interviewee
text: To the estate. And he tells me-- and later on, I found out that he was the biggest antisemite, that if he knew or suspected that I was Jewish, he would be the first one to take me  to Gestapo. And he hired me.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was--

speaker: interviewee
text: This officer. He was a Polish officer who was the secretary of the Major. And he hired me. He really liked me. And-- and I was-- first, I was introduced to the Major. And he said he will interview me. And then he said he already has a little grandson, and that I would be able to study with him German.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we studied together German. And then-- I'll tell you in a minute about that situation. And-- and I knew that the Major recognized that I was Jewish, because he dealt with a lot of Jews and he had a lot of Jewish friends. The Austrian Major?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Austrian-- right-- Major. As a matter of fact, I was told later that every summer before the war, he had his Jewish friends over there. And a lot of Jewish friends. And he was a very, very nice Austrian Major. I have to admit it, because more and more you learn that the Austrians were bigger antisemites than the Germans. But you know, he-- not everybody is the same. This was a wonderful, wonderful family. And so I was going to play with his chil-- with the child, with his boy. I don't remember his name. And I was also going to help in the kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the kitchen was-- I even have the picture of the little girl that I worked with. I slept in the kitchen-- and another girl, who was really a tyrant. And there was-- off the kitchen, there was a little room that another girl slept. But she really was his favorite, and the rumor that, you know, he had a little love affair with her. And she had everything to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she became very close to me. And she told me that I can go into the room anytime I want. No other servant can, but I can go in there. And she doesn't mind if I stay there. As a matter of fact, she said, if I want to sleep with her, I can even sleep with her in the bed. And I didn't want to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what I did, is I-- I was with the little boy all the time. And there was some kind of problem there because his daughter-in-law mar-- was Polish, the Major's daughter-in-law. And she insisted upon bringing him up as a Polish kid.

speaker: interviewee
text: His son was in the army. And I never knew if he was in the Polish army or the German army. I was never told, and I never found out. So he didn't care for his sis-- for his daughter-in-law. So he really wanted someone else who will be-- of course, he was too good to play with the peasants. He liked me. That I will be able to play and study with him together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was very, very nice-- the Major to me. What did you tell him about yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: That a Polish girl had run away from home. Not run away, run away from Poland because there was no job. You couldn't get a job in Poland, and everybody was running to other parts. And on the way, I got so tired I decided to see if I can get a job. Somebody told me I can find a job here.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you think you appeared to them? You'd been living in the forest. You'd been running.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't tell them that.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they saw you. How-- did you clean yourself up before you went to the interview? Do you remember what that was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Did I clean myself up? Probably. I don't even know where. But I didn't even remember. Maybe I cleaned myself up before I entered his place. Because this is not a thing that is-- that's unusual, because a lot of Polish girls ran away. There was no jobs.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a lot of Polish girls ran to Germany. And not everybody could get official-- an official train. I mean, the transportation wasn't working. So everybody was running all over. So it's not that here's an unusual thing, somebody came to look for a job. I'm sure he had tens and tens of girls who came to look for jobs there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And also, even the servants were not shocked to see that somebody is coming. So that wasn't unusual. So anyway, he entertained a lot of Gestapo. So later on, of course, he wanted me to help the other-- Genia was her name who was the main-- and he felt that, of course, I was very, very worried about it, that the Gestapo might start suspecting. And later on, as I told you, I realized that he suspected that I was Jewish, the Major. But since he wa-- had a lot of friends, and so he really wanted to help me. So he wanted me to help when the Gestapo was there. And later on, I couldn't figure out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said to myself, maybe he felt, if someone ever tells the Gestapo, here is proof that he-- because they would be very upset and maybe even killed him, if they heard that he's hiding a Jewish girl. So he had proof. Look, I even had her here when you were here. How would I know? I mean, this kind of thinking that I had.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway, I was there. And once, they came for dinner, and an elderly officer came. And he asked me if I might take a walk with him. I said, yes. And went for a walk. And he was holding me under the arms, and he was treating me differently than anybody else. And he was much older. And he started to show me a picture of his son, and his-- and his son looked like a Jewish guy. And I started to suspect-- because, you know, there were some Jews who went to the-- to the German army under false identity. So they thought this is going to be one way of surviving. So I went for a walk with him, and he started to talk.
